comment.body: This setup is key to master. Together with macro and greeks analysis. Very powerful. But need a framework to work with....... All in all, look how negative the news were running into this low from 5/27. Once it flushed there, have to monitor how price behaves, IV, options flow, etc. And from there you have a well defined setup which will either work or not. And the feedback does not take long to manifest. Once it flushes I like to place a AVWAP from the drops to confirm entry(ies) with stop at lows on a lower TF chart. Then all you have to do is trail your stops..... You can see in larger TF chart there were 2 setups for both last and this Friday. But they are all over the place...... Keep in mind they are very easy to see after the fact. But you need to train yourself to see it as it is building or unfolding to be prepared to pull the trigger with more confidence. Imagine when playing any sport you do visualization exercises previous to your game, so that when the situation arises, your mind already did it, and your body is only reacting to the play. Remember these are high probability setups. Keyword "probability".

comment.body:
Retail bots are said to swarm S&P 500 options at predictable times
SP500 -0.01% | May 31, 2025 12:03 PM ET | 7 Comments
The stereotype of algorithmic trading often conjures images of institutional quants and math PhDs managing high-frequency strategies on Wall Street. But today, a growing number of retail traders are embracing automation, running bots and custom scripts from home setups that mirror the tools once reserved for investment banks, Bloomberg News reported Saturday.
This surge in retail automation, however, is creating visible patterns in the options market. According to data from Cboe Global Markets, retail-driven trades -- especially in zero-day-to-expiration (ODTE) S&P 500 options -- are now clustering at specific times, such as 10:00 a.m., 10:15, 10:30, 11:00 and again at 2:00 p.m. Eastern time.
These patterns suggest synchronized, programmatic activity among individual traders, and open the door for more sophisticated players to potentially take advantage.

comment.body:
Short-term vs long-term
Benjamin Graham stated that in the short term (on a weekly, monthly, or even quarterly basis), markets behave like a "voting machine"—driven by investor sentiment, psychology (like FOMO, herd-mentality, etc.), and short-term capital flows, making prices primarily a function of supply and demand dynamics. This can be manipulated by news and narratives designed to influence market sentiment and volatility bets.
This creates volatility cycles, where volatility becomes either over- or underpriced, attracting the herd into one side (momentum) before bagging those overcrowded positions on them (reversion). This is not a conspiracy nor a deliberate attack (though in some cases, it can be—such as when a company faces insider battles over higher ownership stakes through share accumulation). Rather, it is simply a reflexive effect in the markets, as a result of decreased liquidity due to one-sided positioning.
However, in the long run (longer than a year, even ten years), markets act as a "weighing machine", where prices eventually converge toward the intrinsic value of companies, determined by factors such as earnings, cash flows, and balance sheet strength.
In essence, speculation and emotion can dominate rational valuation in the short term, but intrinsic value tends to prevail over longer periods.

comment.body:
Cumulative treasury buyback 2025 (first image) 
schedule into august https://home.treasury.gov/system/files/221/Tentative-Buyback-Schedule.pdf
Second image - cumulative buyback going back to 2023.

Treasury is pulling supply on the front end which then compresses yields. 2023 had 0 events, and 2024 had 1.
Main takeaways
- Treasury became more active with repurchases going into May and into June
- Huge treasury issuance expires end of June and the treasury projects 850B by end of June (current TGA is at like 400 and the RRP is at like 300B)
Likely will see huge auctions over the next 2 weeks which will spike yields temporarily. Cash will rotate from the RRP into the TGA and empty it and then a mild hit to equities (slightly pullback).  Treasury will likely try to spread out the debt across the yield curve and smooth it out. Problem now is with this issuance do investors want that kind of duration risk with inflation rising. Thats why all the macro stuff form earlier in the year needed to be resolved by now.
